clinical_content.chief_complaint: immediate pain in left shoulder and inability to execute a chip shot
symptoms: pain preventing execution of chip shot
pain with external rotation versus resistance
clinical_content.examination: Jonathan is pleasant and cooperative. Elevation of 170, internal rotation to T6, cross-arm adduction to metacarpophalangeal joints. External rotation with the arm at his side is 85 degrees. External rotation versus resistance is 4/5 and painful. He points to the AC joint as the source of his pain. He has no AC deformity, but he is tender over the apex of the AC joint anteriorly. No scapular winging. Impingement signs are negative. Biceps signs are negative. Teitge maneuver is positive and distinctly so. No signs of instability of glenohumeral ligaments. Negative relocation sign. Negative apprehension sign. Negative sulcus sign. Cervical spine examination is unremarkable. X-rays taken of his shoulder in weighted views and compared to the opposite side show no widening of the AC joint. No superior elevation of the AC joint. It is very symmetric with the opposite side and there is no evidence of any obvious fracture or dislocation.
clinical_content.diagnosis: grade 1, almost a grade 2 AC separation based on his clinical examination
clinical_content.treatment_plan: correct him with an orthoplast-type adjustment to his shoulder pads over the AC joint when he does return that he can tape; continue to skate but avoid any slap shots or any contact until his symptoms resolve
clinical_content.follow_up: another 2-4 weeks before he is fully recovered; If you have any questions, do not hesitate to call.

clinical_content.chief_complaint: pain in his left hand after being hit

Initial strength 3+/5, later improved to 4/5. Pain localized to first metacarpal on dorsal aspect of hand. X-rays negative. Slight swelling along the ulnar aspect of the thumb noted later. Pain focused in adductor and extensor pollicis tendons. Strength of adductor pollicis initially 1/5, increased to 3+/5 and then 4/5. Full ROM and strength of left wrist and hand by 2009-04-02.
clinical_content.diagnosis: Left Other metacarpophalyngeal oxc sprain, carpal-metacarpal sprain
clinical_content.treatment_plan: ICE (15 min), tape job, EMS VMS (15 min), US (5 min, various settings), Thermotherapy (whirlpool/hot pack), range of motion exercises, wrist flexion/extension exercises with weights (2-5 lbs), wrist ulnar/radial deviation exercises with weights (2-5 lbs), pronation/supination exercises with weights (5 lbs), therapist resisted thumb adduction, abduction, flexion, extension, massage of hand and thumb (including CMP ligaments, adductors), splint (fabricated in Detroit, later a new playing splint), isometric exercises, muscle re-education for adductor pollicis, rest and activity modification, bracing for games.
clinical_content.follow_up: Re-evaluate tomorrow morning. X-Ray if necessary initially. Get another opinion if no improvement. Continue strengthening and monitor progress. Event closed on 2009-04-07 after regaining full pain-free function and playing without bracing. Remove brace and continue to monitor.

clinical_content.chief_complaint: recovering from an AC joint sprain in the shoulder; uncomfortable after getting hit on the outside of the left leg and/or knee; pain is all on the outside of the knee; it bothers him when he skates; complaining about his left hand; feels pain in the vicinity of the second and third metacarpal heads and also somewhat along the shaft of the first, second and third metacarpals
clinical_content.examination: hand does show some ill-defined tenderness to palpation about the second and third metacarpal heads; no triggering; no locking; no swelling; no instability at the MP joints; motion is well maintained as is his strength; no snuffbox tenderness; wrist seems to be benign; fingers are pink and warm; tender on the lateral side of the knee; more seemingly soft tissue in the vicinity of the IT band inserting on Gerdy’s; some minor tenderness here as well; proximal fibula seems benign; no distal radicular type symptoms consistent with any peroneal nerve problems; knee shows no swelling; motion is well maintained; collaterals and cruciates are stable; calf is soft; no patellofemoral instability; no obvious fracture on the hand films; x-rays of the knee are unremarkable
clinical_content.diagnosis: Left knee contusion possibly to IT band and Gerdy’s tubercle versus atypical lateral meniscus tear; Hand sprain; AC joint sprain in the shoulder
clinical_content.treatment_plan: keep working with him in the training room
clinical_content.follow_up: get an MRI to verify that there is no intra-articular problem (for knee); set this up for him today

clinical_content.chief_complaint: Left hand pain.
symptoms: discomfort with forceful pass or slap shot
clinical_content.examination: Slightly tender at the index carpometacarpal joint level. Slight increase in discomfort with stressing this joint with passive flexion and extension. Strong radial wrist extension, but slight discomfort with testing the extensor carpi radialis longus against resistance. Finger motion is full. Well-healed scar dorsal and palmar first web space. Some first dorsal interosseous atrophy on a chronic basis. Index MP joint motion is full and stable to varus and valgus stress. Collateral ligaments do not seem bothersome. No extensor tendon dysfunction. Profundus and superficialis are strong. Not tender at the first annular pulley, over the scaphoid or radius, or at the first dorsal compartment. Watson’s maneuver is not bothersome whatsoever. Thumb range of motion is full. X-rays show no obvious acute fracture, degenerative change, or subluxation. Scaphoid looks normal. No signs of fracture or radiocarpal arthritis.
clinical_content.diagnosis: sprain at the index carpometacarpal joint level.
clinical_content.treatment_plan: activity modification as necessary, taping, allow additional time for recovery, continue to participate with taping.
clinical_content.follow_up: See him in another 7 to 10 days if his difficulty persists. Consider additional diagnostic studies (bone scan).

clinical_content.chief_complaint: Jonathan's right shoulder has been sore. Pain and weakness with overhead activity. Was injected into right a/c joint 1 month ago with relief, now states pain is in different location.
symptoms: difficulty to move arm over head
clinical_content.examination: Right shoulder full ROM. Tender anteriorly over coracoid process, and long head of biceps. Strength normal in all muscle groups. Positive impingement sign. No instability or apprehension. MRI today revealed AC joint arthritis, possible degenerative tearing of the labrum. Decreased overhead motion due to pain. Decreased pain in pressing.
clinical_content.diagnosis: Long head of biceps tendinitis; AC joint arthritis; possible degenerative tearing of the labrum.
clinical_content.treatment_plan: Rotator cuff program. Intra-articular injection. Manual therapy (massage). Tubing/Bands exercises. Laser therapy (Deep Chronic Pain, Deep Chronic Inflammation). Ice. Soft tissue work. Range of motion exercises. Core stabilization exercise.
clinical_content.follow_up: Monitor symptoms. Consult with Dr. Plagens as needed. Re-evaluate.

clinical_content.chief_complaint: right shoulder pain
clinical_content.examination: No os acromiale or Hill-Sachs deformity of the humeral head. Long head of the biceps tendon is intact and seen in the bicipital groove. Acromioclavicular joint demonstrates a small effusion with subchondral edema involving the distal clavicle and the acromion. The superior and inferior acromioclavicular ligaments appear intact. Acromioclavicular joint is not widened. Mild thickening and increased signal of rotator cuff tendons related to mild tendinosis and intrasubstance tear. No focal full-thickness tear. Blunting of the superior glenoid labrum is suggestive of a degenerative SLAP tear (superior labrum anterior-posterior). Minimal amount of fluid in the glenohumeral joint is likely physiologic.
clinical_content.diagnosis: Mild rotator cuff tendinosis; Findings suggestive of prior grade 1 sprain of the acromioclavicular joint without evidence of instability; Subchondral edema of the distal clavicle and acromion with mild degenerative changes; Degenerative SLAP tear.

clinical_content.treatment_plan: one per day with food
one intra-articular injection in the glenohumeral joint of the right shoulder. 12 mg total dose

clinical_content.treatment_plan: Open reduction internal fixation, left scaphoid waist fracture; Use and interpretation of intraoperative fluoroscopy; Application of short-arm plaster splint; Supraclavicular block with IV sedation; IV antibiotics; Curvilinear incision made; Identified third dorsal extensor compartment; Radialized EPL tendon; Opened dorsal capsule; Copiously irrigated and suctioned joint; Inserted guidewire; Used primary and secondary drill to drill proximal cortex; Used 26-mm standard Acutrak 2 screw for compression; Removed guidewire; Closed dorsal joint capsule with #3-0 Ethibond suture; Closed extensor retinaculum with #3-0 Ethibond suture; Closed subcutaneous tissue with #4-0 Vicryl suture; Closed skin with #4-0 Prolene in running subcuticular pullout stitch; Cleansed hand with hydrogen peroxide solution; Mastisol and Steri-Strips applied to wound; Bacitracin ointment, Adaptic gauze, and 4x4 fluffs applied; Placed patient into short-arm thumb spica splint
